nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline zones of Lake Tanganyika; found among and immediately above rocky substrate at moderate depths, often associated with shell-free rock piles and sediment patches between boulders

decorPreferences: stacked rocks and cave formations to define territories
sandy substrate (fine aragonite or crushed coral to buffer pH)
open sandy areas between rock structures
minimal planting — Vallisneria or Java fern acceptable but not required
flat rock slates for spawning sites

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding primarily on small invertebrates, zooplankton, and crustaceans in the water column and substrate. In the aquarium accepts high-quality small cichlid pellets, frozen cyclops, frozen brine shrimp, frozen daphnia, and mysis shrimp. Live foods (baby brine shrimp, micro-worms) are relished and beneficial for conditioning. Vegetable-based or algae-heavy foods are generally best avoided, as this species is adapted to a strictly animal-based diet.

goodWith: other Tanganyika rock-dwellers of differing habitat zones (e.g., Julidochromis species, Tropheus if tank is large)
Synodontis catfish (S. multipunctatus, S. petricola) as dither/cleanup fish
Altolamprologus calvus or compressiceps (non-competing rock zone)
Tanganyika shell-dwellers in separate tank zones if space permits
adult conspecifics in established groups of 6+ with adequate territory
avoidWith: aggressive Mbuna cichlids (incompatible water chemistry and aggression mismatch)
small tetras, livebearers, or nano fish (will be predated or harassed)
unrelated cichlids sharing identical territory type
newly introduced conspecifics into an established group without adequate territory

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Neolamprologus pulcher; standard reference photo showing typical coloration.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult coloration with iridescent blue-white fin margins; good representative photo.

summary: The Princess Cichlid is a small Lake Tanganyika species famed for cooperative breeding, in which non-breeding offspring remain as helpers to defend the territory and assist with later clutches. Locality forms include the yellow Daffodil and dark-patterned Black Princess. Requires hard, alkaline water and structured rock territories.
careNotes:
The Princess Cichlid is endemic to the rocky coastal zones of Lake Tanganyika and requires hard, alkaline water — pH 7.8–9.0, gH 10–20, kH 10–18 — to remain healthy. Tanganyika buffer salts or a crushed coral substrate are commonly used to maintain water chemistry stability. Temperature should be held in the 24–27 °C range, as fluctuations beyond this tend to stress the immune system. A minimum 150 L tank with a 100 cm footprint is recommended for a breeding pair; significantly more space — 200–300 L or larger — is needed to house a full cooperative group of 6–12 individuals.

The aquascape should prioritise dense rock work: stacked stones forming caves and crevices allow the dominant breeding pair and helper fish to establish distinct micro-territories. A fine aragonite sand substrate at least 5 cm deep provides a natural pH buffer and reflects the rocky Tanganyika shoreline. Open sandy areas between rock formations are appreciated and closely match wild conditions.

The Princess Cichlid is most rewarding when kept as a colony. When raised as a group, juveniles naturally remain with their parents and assist in fry defense, territory guarding, and egg-fanning — a behaviour that has made this species among the most intensively studied models in evolutionary biology research on kin selection and cooperative behaviour. Dominant breeders may evict or harass lower-ranked helpers, so tank length must be sufficient for subordinate fish to withdraw from aggression.

Several locality variants are found in the hobby. The 'Daffodil' form from Zambia shows striking yellow coloration; the 'Burundi' form is more silver-blue; and 'Black Princess' specimens display dark melanistic patterning.

Neolamprologus pulcher is closely related to N. brichardi (commonly sold as the Fairy Cichlid or Lyretail Cichlid), and the two are sometimes treated as the same species. The Princess Cichlid is distinguished by blue spots at the base of the gill cover and on the cheek, and by a slightly more stocky build compared to the more elongated N. brichardi. The two should not be kept together. Within an established colony, conspecific aggression is generally managed through territory structure; introducing new adults into an established group risks severe aggression and should be accompanied by a full rearrangement of the tank.
breedingNotes:
The Princess Cichlid is a biparental, substrate-spawning cichlid that forms a stable monogamous pair bond. The breeding pair excavates a spawning site beneath or within a flat rock crevice, and the female deposits 50–150 adhesive eggs that are guarded intensely by both parents. Incubation takes approximately 2–3 days at 26 °C, with free-swimming fry emerging around 7–10 days post-hatch.

The most distinctive aspect of this species is cooperative breeding: offspring from previous clutches remain in the group as helpers and actively assist in territory defense and fry protection — a behaviour extensively studied as a model of kin-selected altruism in vertebrates. Helpers may eventually inherit territory or breed as subordinates within the group. In the aquarium, keeping the colony intact substantially improves fry survival rates and reduces pressure from other tank inhabitants.

Brood sizes are modest, and multiple clutches per year are typical in well-maintained conditions. Fry are large enough at the free-swimming stage to accept baby brine shrimp and micro-pellets. Spawning itself is generally straightforward once a bonded pair is established, though the need for precise water chemistry and careful territorial management makes overall breeding difficulty moderate.

primary.notes: Primary source. additional.notes: UK trade publication species profile; confirmed aquarium size recommendations, decor requirements, diet (carnivore, small frozen foods), lifespan (up to 10 years reported in good conditions), and breeding notes including cooperative helper behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Monogamous substrate spawner that deposits 50–150 adhesive eggs per clutch in a flat rock crevice, with both parents and any colony helpers guarding actively. Fry are typically free-swimming within around 10 days. The cooperative breeding colony — in which offspring from earlier clutches remain as helpers — is the natural social system for this species, and maintaining the group intact tends to improve breeding success considerably. Breeding difficulty is moderate overall, primarily due to the water chemistry precision and territorial management required.
